danger_signals.meaning: Player is approaching financial elimination from the game

danger_signals.visual_form: Rent payment animation or currency transfer visual
danger_signals.meaning: Active financial loss state; immediate resource drain

danger_signals.visual_form: Token repositioning to jail space with visual indicator
danger_signals.meaning: Temporary movement restriction; potential missed opportunities

safety_signals.visual_form: Color-coded property indicator on board tiles
safety_signals.meaning: Secured asset generating income; protected territory

safety_signals.visual_form: Currency counter visible on player HUD
safety_signals.meaning: Financial security and flexibility to make purchases or pay debts

safety_signals.visual_form: Dedicated board space with safe landing designation
safety_signals.meaning: Risk-free turn with no financial consequences

faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Property color set assigned exclusively to player

faction_indicators.color_identity: Typically yellow or secondary controller color
faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Unique game piece token distinct from Player 1

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: May have visual indicator denoting computer control
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: AI-claimed properties marked with assigned color

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Non-playable entity managing currency and property deeds
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Central repository for unowned properties and cards

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Board appears mostly uncolored; sparse property ownership indicators
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Players move freely; negotiation and acquisition phase active

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Board shows increasing property color clustering; multiple color sets visible
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Strategic property trading; calculated movement; income generation begins

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Board dominated by complete color sets with house/hotel indicators; visual wealth disparity obvious
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: High rent payments; reduced player flexibility; elimination phase begins

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Target player token visually diminished or grayed; cash display critically low; few properties remain
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Forced property sales; inability to pay rents; final turns before bankruptcy

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Dominant player shows colorful property monopolies with houses/hotels; largest cash pool displayed
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Control over board movement; collection of substantial rents; opponent elimination trigger

environmental_storytelling.example: Visual concentration of same-colored properties on board indicates player control and economic power concentration
Visual building structures on properties communicate investment level and rent multiplication power
Visible disparity in player cash reserves tells economic inequality story without text
Animation pace may indicate player turn order or game phase progression
Chance and Community Chest card visual indicators show upcoming decision points and fortune variables

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Follows dice roll path; stops on landing space; triggers rent/acquisition logic
Pause animation; visible calculation indicator; property purchase or trade initiation
Neutral color; available for purchase; displayed in auction or purchase menu
Displays owner color; generates income on landing; accepts house/hotel construction
Currency animation flows from landing player to owner; cash display updates visibly

notes: Monopoly SNES (1991) follows the standard board game paradigm digitized for console play. The game communicates world-state primarily through property color ownership, cash display differentials, and board position. Faction identity is represented through distinct player tokens and color-coded property sets. Danger is signaled through low cash reserves and high-rent properties; safety through liquidity and owned property income generation. The 1991 console version would have used simple 2D graphics and sprite-based tokens, with turn-based mechanics creating natural decision points. Computer AI opponents require visual indicators of their economic status and strategic choices. The paradigm classification as 'puzzle' appears inaccurate—this is a strategy/digital tabletop game—but reflects source data provided.
sources_used: wikipedia - Monopoly video games (console and computer versions section)
